zotero.abstractNote: Nonlinear representation learning is a fundamental task in machine learning, with applications ranging from causal discovery to domain adaptation. A central question is whether the learned representations are identifiable, i.e., whether they recover the underlying ground-truth factors of variation. While recent work has established conditions for identifiability, these often rely on strong assumptions about the data-generating process. In this paper, we study the robustness of nonlinear representation learning to violations of these assumptions. We show that even when the data-generating process deviates from the assumed model, the learned representations can still be robust in the sense that they remain close to the ground-truth factors. We provide theoretical guarantees for this robustness and demonstrate our findings on synthetic and real-world data.
zotero.proceedingsTitle: Proceedings of the 41st International Conference on Machine Learning
